senses.definition: Exact photographic prints or copies made by an early printing process.
examples: The museum keeps several autotypes of the original paintings in storage.
Old books sometimes include autotypes instead of real photographs.

senses.definition: The process of giving a person a small amount of typhoid bacteria to make them resistant to the disease.
examples: Early experiments with autotyphization raised serious safety concerns.
Autotyphization is now of only historical interest in immunology.

senses.definition: Relating to autotypes, especially early photographic reproductions.
examples: The archive contains autotypic plates of nineteenth‑century portraits.
Autotypic printing was common in early illustrated journals.

senses.definition: Processes or instances of making autotypes, especially in early photographic printing.
examples: Nineteenth‑century autotypies are fragile and must be stored carefully.
He studied various autotypies to compare printing quality.

senses.definition: The process or technique of making autotypes, especially in early photography and printing.
examples: Autotyping allowed books to include detailed photographic illustrations.
The workshop demonstrated nineteenth‑century autotyping for visitors.

senses.definition: An early printing process that combines photographic images with typographic elements using autotype methods.
examples: Autotypography transformed the look of illustrated magazines.
Researchers study autotypography to understand the history of image reproduction.

senses.definition: The process of producing autotypes, especially early photographic reproductions.
examples: Autotypy made it possible to reproduce photographs in books cheaply.
The principles of autotypy influenced later printing technologies.

senses.definition: Relating to a treatment or vaccine prepared from a person's own urine, especially in older medical practice.
examples: The doctor mentioned an autourine preparation in a historical case study.
Autourine remedies are now regarded as unscientific and risky.

senses.definition: A vaccine made from bacteria taken from the same person or animal that will receive it.
examples: The veterinarian prepared an autovac to treat the recurring infection.
Early researchers experimented with autovacs for chronic skin conditions.

senses.definition: The act or process of vaccinating someone with a vaccine made from their own bacteria or tissues.
examples: Autovaccination was once tried for stubborn bacterial infections.
Clinical reports described mixed results from autovaccination.

senses.definition: A vaccine made from bacteria or tissues taken from the same person or animal that will receive it.
examples: The patient received an autovaccine prepared from his own bacterial strain.
Autovaccines are rarely used in modern mainstream medicine.

senses.definition: Vaccines made from the recipient’s own bacteria or tissues.
examples: Doctors of that era often relied on autovacs for chronic infections.
Reports compared autovacs with standard commercial vaccines.

senses.definition: A device or system that automatically parks or looks after a car without a human valet.
examples: The new garage features an autovalet that finds a parking space for you.
Engineers are testing autovalet systems for busy city centers.

senses.definition: A valve that opens and closes automatically in response to pressure or flow.
examples: The pump is fitted with an autovalve to prevent backflow.
An autovalve reduces the need for manual control in the system.

senses.definition: The act or process of performing a cutting or experimental operation on one’s own body.
examples: The surgeon’s autovivisection shocked his colleagues and the public.
Autovivisection is usually mentioned only in extreme historical cases.

senses.definition: A device on a camera that advances the film automatically after each shot.
examples: He attached an autowinder to his film camera to speed up shooting.
The built‑in autowinder made the camera popular with sports photographers.

senses.definition: Devices that advance camera film automatically after each exposure.
examples: Older professional cameras often came with separate autowinders.
Collectors sometimes look for original autowinders to match the camera body.

senses.definition: A person who works in a factory that makes cars.
examples: The company announced new benefits for every autoworker on the assembly line.
As an autoworker, she worried about robots replacing her job.

senses.definition: People who work in car factories.
examples: Thousands of autoworkers went on strike over pay and conditions.
The deal was welcomed by autoworkers across the country.

senses.definition: The need of a parasite to use different host species at different stages of its life cycle.
examples: Autoxeny complicates attempts to control certain parasitic worms.
The textbook briefly explains autoxeny in the life cycle of the parasite.

senses.definition: A slow chemical reaction where a substance reacts with oxygen in the air without needing external energy.
examples: Autoxidation causes oils to become rancid over time.
Researchers added antioxidants to delay autoxidation in the product.

senses.definition: Processes in which substances react slowly with oxygen in the air without added energy.
examples: Different autoxidations can damage plastics, fuels, and foods.
The paper compares several autoxidations in organic compounds.

senses.definition: A substance or device that promotes or measures autoxidation.
examples: The lab used an autoxidator to test how quickly the oil became rancid.
Adding an autoxidator accelerated the reaction for experimental purposes.

senses.definition: The degree to which a substance is able to undergo autoxidation.
examples: The autoxidizability of the fat increases with temperature.
They measured autoxidizability to predict shelf life.

senses.definition: Able to react slowly with oxygen in the air by autoxidation.
examples: Highly unsaturated fats are particularly autoxidizable.
Storing autoxidizable materials in air causes them to degrade.

senses.definition: To react slowly with oxygen in the air without added energy.
examples: These oils autoxidize quickly if left in open containers.
At high temperatures the polymer may autoxidize and become brittle.

senses.definition: A substance that easily undergoes autoxidation or promotes autoxidation in other materials.
examples: The compound acts as a strong autoxidizer in the mixture.
Handling an autoxidizer requires careful storage away from air and light.

senses.definition: A feeding individual in a colonial animal, especially in bryozoans, that is responsible for getting food.
examples: Each autozooid filters tiny particles from the water.
The colony contained both autozooids and specialized non‑feeding zooids.

senses.definition: Other, used in some fixed phrases borrowed from French.
examples: The novel explores her search for an autre self.
Critics praised the film for its sense of l’autre, the radically different other.

senses.definition: In former times; long ago, used especially in legal or literary English.
examples: The doctrine of autrefois acquit prevents a person being tried twice for the same crime.
The castle, autrefois a royal residence, now stands in ruins.

senses.definition: The season between summer and winter, when the weather becomes cooler and leaves fall from trees.
examples: We usually go hiking in autumn when the leaves are colorful.
In this country, autumn starts in September and ends in November.

senses.definition: The later part of someone’s life or of a process, when it is past its peak but still has value.
examples: She wrote her best books in the autumn of her career.
The village seemed to be in the autumn of its existence, peaceful but fading.

senses.definition: Relating to autumn, especially its colors, feelings, or weather.
examples: The park has an autumnal beauty with golden leaves everywhere.
We enjoyed the autumnal chill in the early morning air.

senses.definition: In a way that is like autumn or reminds you of autumn.
examples: The sky glowed autumnally over the quiet fields.
The city felt autumnally calm after the busy summer season.

senses.definition: Relating to or characteristic of autumn, used rarely and mainly in literary style.
examples: He wrote of the autumnian light over the hills.
The poem captures an autumnian mood of quiet change.

senses.definition: The quality or feeling of autumn, especially in a poetic description.
examples: The painting has a deep autumnity that many viewers find moving.
A sense of autumnity runs through the last chapter of the novel.

senses.definition: Plural of autumn; more than one autumn season.
examples: We spent many autumns walking in these woods together.
The climate here brings mild autumns and cold winters.

senses.definition: Having a feeling, color, or weather like autumn.
examples: It was a cool, autumny afternoon in the park.
The soup has a warm, autumny taste with pumpkin and spices.

senses.definition: Relating to the Autunian, an early stage of the Permian period in geology.
examples: The fossils were found in Autunian rock formations.
Researchers compared Autunian layers from different regions.

senses.definition: A yellow‑green radioactive mineral that contains uranium and is used as a minor ore of uranium.
examples: The sample of autunite glowed green under ultraviolet light.
Autunite often forms thin, square crystals in uranium‑rich rocks.

senses.definition: Pieces or crystals of the mineral autunite.
examples: The museum display includes several bright autunites.
Geologists collected autunites from the old uranium mine.

senses.definition: Independent creative or productive activity carried out by oneself, sometimes used in philosophical writing.
examples: The philosopher described human freedom as a form of auturgy.
Art can be seen as auturgy, the self’s work upon itself.

senses.definition: A socket, cable, or audio input used to connect external sound devices, short for auxiliary.
examples: I plugged my phone into the car’s aux to play music.
The speaker has Bluetooth and an aux on the back.

senses.definition: Short for auxiliary; used of extra or supporting equipment or workers.
examples: The aux staff were called in during the emergency.
They installed an aux pump as a backup.

senses.definition: An enzyme preparation that helps break down starch, used in laboratory or industrial processes.
examples: Adding auxamylase speeds up the conversion of starch to sugars.
The protocol requires a small amount of auxamylase solution.

senses.definition: A record or chart showing how an organism grows under different conditions.
examples: The auxanogram illustrates the bacteria’s growth at various temperatures.
Researchers drew an auxanogram to compare nutrient effects on growth.

senses.definition: The scientific study of growth in plants, animals, or microorganisms.
examples: Auxanology provides tools to analyze how cells increase in size.
Her research in auxanology focuses on bacterial growth patterns.

senses.definition: An instrument used to measure the growth of plants, especially changes in length.
examples: The auxanometer recorded the daily growth of the seedlings.
Using an auxanometer, they tracked how light affected stem growth.

senses.definition: Instruments used to measure plant growth, especially changes in length.
examples: Different auxanometers were tested for accuracy in the greenhouse.
Modern sensors have largely replaced traditional auxanometers.

senses.definition: Instances or processes of growth or increase in size.
examples: The study compares auxeses in different plant tissues.
Rapid auxeses can put stress on developing structures.

senses.definition: An increase in size or growth of a cell, tissue, or organism.
examples: Cell auxesis continues until the organism reaches maturity.
The graph shows a period of rapid auxesis followed by slower growth.

senses.definition: A rhetorical device where something is described as greater or more important than it really is.
examples: In this speech, auxesis reinforces the hero’s achievements.
Writers sometimes use auxesis to build emotional intensity.

senses.definition: Describing a material that becomes thicker when it is stretched, rather than thinner as most materials do.
examples: Auxetic materials could improve protective clothing and helmets.
The team tested a new auxetic foam for use in sports equipment.
